coredata.dc:title: The high electron occupation on anti-bond orbital induced by the asymmetric covalent structure of C-S-N enhancing the oxygen reduction reaction
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Lin
Ji, Chunyu
Li, Xueyan
Liu, Zirui
Yang, Hao
Qiu, Feng
Peng, Zhili
Liu, Qingju
Zhang, Longzhou
coredata.dc:description: Despite heteroatom doping is a smart approach to facilitate the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) performances of nanocarbon catalysts, two dominant issues still retain as significant obstacles. One is the relatively low activity compared to benchmark catalysts, the other is the ambiguous elucidation of the catalytic mechanism for dual heteroatom-doped catalysts. Here, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon-dots electrocatalyst (HT-CDs) are simply prepared by means of physical activation with water vapor on the carbon-dots precursor. The material exhibits excellent ORR performance under alkaline conditions, achieving a half-wave potential of 0.91 V (vs RHE), surpassing most metal-free catalysts and Pt/C. Further theoretical studies reveal that N/C-S-N structure fills more electrons in the anti-bond orbitals that promoting the charge transfer from the catalyst to the reactant O2, thus enhancing the efficient ORR process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots (CDs)
Heteroatom doping
Oxygen reduction reaction
Anti-bond orbital
Zn−air batteries

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52362043; 22001228), the \u201CDouble-First Class\u201D University Construction Project (No. C176220100022) and the Yunnan Fundamental Research Project (No. 202201AT070227; 202301AT070147; 202301BF070001-003). Longzhou Zhang also thanks Xingdian Youth Talent Support Program of Yunnan Province (YNQR-QNRC-2018-056). The authors would like to thank the Advance Analysis and Measurement Center of Yunnan University and the Advanced Computing Center of Yunnan University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Flow chart of the preparation of HT-CDs-1 catalyst. (b–e) HAADF-STEM images of HT-CDs-1 at different resolutions. (f) Elemental mappings of C (green), N (yellow) and S (orange) elements from HT-CDs-1. (g–i) HRTEM images of HT-CDs-1, The original greyscale zone marked by dash line in (h) was mapped onto the color-gradient image in (i). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) FT-IR spectra of OPD, CDs-1 and HT-CDs-1 catalysts. (b) XRD patterns of CDs-1 and HT-CDs-1 catalysts. (c) Raman patterns of HT-CDs-1, HT-CDs-2 and HT-CDs-3 catalysts. (d) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms and pore size distribution of HT-CDs-1 catalysts.

(a) ORR polarization curves, with insets showing the corresponding onset potential histograms, (b) Tafel slope plots for HT-CDs-1, HT-CDs-2, HT-CDs-3 and commercial Pt/C catalysts. (c) Electron transfer numbers and H2O2 yield (left), polarization curves at different speeds from 400 to 2500 rpm, inset shows the corresponding K-L curves at different potentials (right) of HT-CDs-1. (d) Comparative J
k and E
1/2 histograms at 0.85 V vs RHE. (e) Comparison of HT-CDs-1 and reported ORR electrocatalysts in terms of J
k and E
1/2 . (f) Electrochemical stability of HT-CDs-1 and commercial Pt/C catalysts.

(a) ORR polarization curves and (b) Tafel slope plots of HT-CDs-1 and control catalysts. (c) N 1s and (d) S 2p high-resolution XPS patterns, (e) and (f) Comparative plots of the chemical valence states of N and S for HT-CDs-1, HT-CDs-2 and HT-CDs-3 catalysts, respectively. (g) Molecular orbital energy level diagrams for the three structures C-S-C, N-S-N and C-S-N.

(a) The models of atomic configuration of the three structures C-S-C, C-S-N and N-S-N. Carbon atoms, nitrogen atoms and sulfur atoms are represented by the grey, blue and yellow balls respectively. (b) Top view of the charge distribution of the three structures C-S-C, C-S-N and N-S-N. The negative charges and positive charges concentrated areas are marked by red and blue zones respectively. (c–d) Top view of the electron occupation of the HOMO and LUMO for C-S-C, C-S-N and N-S-N. The yellow zone is the HOMO/LUMO electron filling area, while the blue zone is the HOMO/LUMO hole filling area. (e) The models of valence electron filling on the molecular orbitals of C-S-C, C-S-N and N-S-N, and the calculated energy level values of the corresponding valence electron orbitals. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Performance of Zn-Air batteries with HT-CDs-1 catalysts as air cathodes: (a) open circuit voltage and time correspondence plot, (b) step discharge curve, (c) discharge polarization curve and power density curve, (d) constant current discharge curve at different current densities, (e) charge/discharge cycle curve at 1 mA cm−2 current density.

head.ce:title: The high electron occupation on anti-bond orbital induced by the asymmetric covalent structure of C-S-N enhancing the oxygen reduction reaction

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this article.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Despite heteroatom doping is a smart approach to facilitate the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) performances of nanocarbon catalysts, two dominant issues still retain as significant obstacles. One is the relatively low activity compared to benchmark catalysts, the other is the ambiguous elucidation of the catalytic mechanism for dual heteroatom-doped catalysts. Here, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon-dots electrocatalyst (HT-CDs) are simply prepared by means of physical activation with water vapor on the carbon-dots precursor. The material exhibits excellent ORR performance under alkaline conditions, achieving a half-wave potential of 0.91 V (vs RHE), surpassing most metal-free catalysts and Pt/C. Further theoretical studies reveal that N/C-S-N structure fills more electrons in the anti-bond orbitals that promoting the charge transfer from the catalyst to the reactant O2, thus enhancing the efficient ORR process.

The electron occupation alteration of the anti-bond orbital by N, S co-dopant on carbon dots accelerates the ORR performance in alkaline environments.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fuel cells and metal-air batteries are current renewable energy devices that effectively mitigate issues related to energy scarcity, electrochemical energy storage and environmental pollution [1–6]. The development of catalysts with high electrochemical reactivity to enhance the energy conversion efficiency of these devices has been a consensus. In recent years, metal-free carbon-based nanomaterials (CNMs) have been considered as promising alternatives to precious metal (Pt)-based catalysts due to their unique electronic properties and economic advantages [7–10]. Efficient electrocatalysts have been elaborated by doping CNMs with heteroatoms (such as B, N, P, S, and Cl, etc.). Numerous studies attribute significant improvements in the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) performance of CNMs to their rich multilayer pore structure, high specific surface area, as well as to topological defects (e.g., non-hexagonal carbon polygons), vacancies (removal of heteroatoms) and heteroatom sites (such as marginal thiophene S, pyridinic and graphitic N) in carbon materials [7,9,11–18].

Among various heteroatoms doped so far, N and S doped materials stand out in metal-free electrocatalysts for ORR applications due to their diverse number of active sites and synergistic effects. However, the active sites of N and S doped materials in ORR are still subject to much controversy, among which the following four prevailing opinions are: (1) S atoms in the graphite lattice (C-S-C species) [19–22], (2) pyridinic or graphitic nitrogen [20,22,23], (3) lattice defects (vacancies and dislocations) [22–24], (4) N-S-C or S-N species [7,20]. For example, Lei et al. constructed S-rich doped carbon nitride (C3N4) nanoparticles (S-C3N4/CNT) on carbon nanotubes (CNT). The article proposed that S doping can effectively increase the lattice defects (vacancies and dislocations) of C3N4, thus improving its electrical conductivity and specific surface area. At the same time, S doping can also promote the transformation of pyrrole-type nitrogen to pyridyl-type nitrogen, thus increasing its proton acceptor density and the number of active sites. From the perspective of the electronic structure, the underlying nature of the ORR mechanism towards N, S co-doped carbon is the energy level and electron occupation of the valence orbital, which determine the binding strength and the overall reactivity of the catalysts [25]. Nevertheless, the specific effect of N, S co-doping is still unveiled, and more discussion is highly desired.

Here, we utilized carbon dots (CDs) rich in N and S heteroatoms as precursors to fabricate the N, S co-doped carbon catalyst. A series of thiophenic S and graphitic N co-doped carbon dots catalysts (HT-CDs) were obtained via hydrothermal and water vapor physical activation methods. The spherical aberration-corrected transmission electron microscopy (AC-TEM) demonstrates the pentagon ring contributed by the N, S heteroatoms on the carbon edges. The art of the state catalyst HT-CDs-1 exhibited superior ORR performance with a half-wave potential as high as 0.91 V, surpassing that of commercial Pt/C catalysts in alkaline electrolytes [16,26–28]. The N/C-S-N structure in the catalyst fills more electrons into the πg molecular anti-bond orbital compared to C-S-C structure, attributing to the weaker adsorption of H2O, herein achieving the high ORR activity. Moreover, the solid-state zinc-air battery assembled by HT-CDs-1 also presented the high energy density (813.6 Wh/kg) and satisfied stability (108 h).

Ethanol (C2H6O), isopropanol (C3H8O) and potassium hydroxide (KOH) with a specification of 98 % were purchased from Tianjin Windship Chemical Reagent Technology Company (Tianjin, China); Nafion with a specification of 5 wt% was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich; and platinum carbon catalyst (Pt/C) with a specification of 20 wt% was purchased from JM (Aladdin).

To prepare HT-CDs, we followed a rigorous protocol. Initially, 100 mg of CDs were transferred to a 50 mL beaker. Subsequently, 10 mL of ethanol and 20 mL of ultrapure water were added, followed by sonication for 1 h to ensure homogenous dispersion. The mixture was then poured into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave of 100 mL capacity and heated in an oven at 180 °C for 12 h. After the autoclave cooled to room temperature, the solution was transferred to a 100 ml round bottom flask and gently rotated. Next, 85 mg of the sample was weighed into a quartz boat and set aside. At the same time, 3 mL of ultrapure water was removed with a pipette gun and placed in the other quartz boat. The two quartz boats were placed together in the center of the tube furnace. The boats were then heated to 900 °C at a rate of 5 °C per minute under a nitrogen atmosphere. The boats were held at 900 °C for 2 h and then cooled to room temperature, yielding the desired catalysts, which were labeled as HT-CDs. Preparation of control (non-doped) catalysts, HT-CDs-S and HT-CDs-N: the synthesis method and reaction conditions were the same as those for HT-CDs. The catalysts in this work were all synthesised in the same way, differing only in the CDs used, i.e. the precursors were replaced during the synthesis of the catalysts.

Field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were captured using a Zeiss Gemini 500 instrument with a voltage setting of 5.00 kV to observe the surface morphology of the catalyst. A transmission electron microscope (Talos F200X) was employed to analyze various aspects of particle morphology, microstructure and crystal structure in the samples. The surface tiny morphology and nano-structure of the catalysts were analyzed using a 300 kV spherical aberration corrected electron microscopy (Thermofisher Spectra 300). The instrument used for IR was a Varian 640-IR, and a KBr pressed sample preparation method was used to determine the chemisorbed species and functional group composition on the catalyst surface. The instrument used for Raman spectroscopy (Raman) was inVia. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was tested on instrument K-Alpha+ with precise charge correction of the data based on the C 1s (284.8 eV).

Weigh 2 mg of the sample into a 2 mL microcentrifuge tube and add 350 μL of isopropanol, 150 μL of ultrapure water, and 40 μL of Nafion. Sonicate the mixture for 30 min until the sample is uniformly dispersed and then set it aside. Next, polish the glassy carbon electrode and clean it with Al2O3 until it achieves a mirror surface. Rinse and dry the electrode with ultrapure water. Following this, pipette a 24 μL drop of catalyst ink onto the glassy carbon electrode and allow it to dry at room temperature. The diameter of the glassy carbon electrode used was 4 mm, resulting in a catalyst loading on the working electrode of 0.588 mg/cm2. Commercial catalysts Pt/C (20 wt%) were compared with the same loading. All electrocatalytic-related tests were performed at room temperature using an electrochemical workstation (CHI 760E), a rotating disc electrode (RDE), and a rotating ring disc electrode (RRDE) in a standard three-electrode system. The system included a glassy carbon working electrode coated with catalyst ink, an Ag/AgCl reference electrode, and a carbon rod counter electrode. All electrode potentials were converted through the Nernst equation (ERHE = EAg/AgCl + 0.197 + 0.059 pH) relative to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE). All tested curve data (except electrochemical activity area plots) were corrected for IR compensation.

The ORR performance was evaluated in oxygen-saturated 0.1 M KOH electrolyte. The ORR test was conducted using linear scanning voltammetry (LSV) at a scan rate of 5 mV/s over a potential range of 0.2-(-0.7)V with respect to Ag/AgCl. The current-potential curves were plotted at different rotation rates (400, 625, 900, 1225, 1600, 2025 and 2500 rpm) with the same scan rate of 5 mV/s. The electron transfer number (n) and the hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) yield during the ORR were determined by LSV for a rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE) tested at 1600 rpm with a scan rate of 5 mV/s. The Tafel slope was derived by fitting the corresponding ORR reaction curves, plotting the logarithm of the current density against the potential. The electrochemical active area (ECSA) was determined by calculating the double layer capacitance (Cdl) of the catalyst through cyclic voltammetry (CV) tests conducted at sweep rates of 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 mV/s within the non-Faraday interval. The horizontal axis represents the different scanning rates, while the vertical axis represents the absolute value of the current difference corresponding to the intermediate potential. A scatter plot was plotted and fitted linearly, with the slope representing the Cdl of the catalyst. The catalysts were tested at constant potential using the timed current method. Carbon rods were used instead of platinum wire counter electrodes to exclude the effect of platinum on the wire being reduced to the catalyst surface.
In this work, the first-principle calculations were performed using Gaussian. The wave function was described using the Hartree-Fock method, and the basis set used was 6-31G.

The CDs were prepared using o-phenylenediamine (OPD), m-phenylenediamine (MPD) and p-phenylenediamine (PPD) as carbon precursors, denoted as CDs-1, CDs-2, and CDs-3, respectively, referring the synthesis in our previous article [29]. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization proved that the uniform dispersion of CDs-1 on the support, with an average size of 2.57 nm (Fig. S1). Zoom-in image further presents the lattice spacing of CDs-1 as 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon (Fig. S1). The distinct spikes on Raman spectroscopy feature at 1263 cm−1 and 1363 cm−1 are attributed to C-N bending vibrations. The peaks at 1471 cm−1 and 1514 cm−1 are attributed to C-C and C=C stretching vibration peaks on the benzene ring (Fig. S2) [30].

The following fabrication demonstration is shown in Fig. 1 a. The obtained CDs mixture was hydrothermally heated to 180 °C for 12 h. Then the collected dark purple powder was transferred to a quartz boat and activated by the water vapor under 900 °C for 2 h in the furnace. The resulting catalysts are designated as HT-CDs-X (X = 1, 2, 3, N and S) respectively. To investigate the effectiveness of steam etching to the morphology, field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was also conducted. From Fig. S3, it can be visualized that the CDs particles underwent dramatic aggregation and formed stacks resembling large carbon spheres or nanosheets, resulting in an overall sparse and fluffy morphological structure.

The HT-CDs-1 were observed to disperse within a cross-linked graphene-like carbon network structure from the spherical aberration corrected scanning transmission electron microscopy (AC-STEM) (Fig. 1b-e). Specifically, under the atomic resolution bright spots indicating the element heavier than carbon were clearly witnessed on the carbon substrate. Each atoms of these dopants was uniformly and individually dispersed. Further EDS elemental mapping images (Fig. 1f) and spectrum (Fig. S4) unveiled that in addition to nitrogen, the sulfur was also doped onto the CDs which was induced during the sulfuric acid treatment in the fabrication of CDs. Therefrom, it could be deduced that the dispersive bright spots with high contrast correspond to the doped sulfur atoms. In aiming to further unveil the atomic structure and avoid structure disrupting by the converging electron beam, the image spherical aberration corrected TEM was applied. It reveals a fascinating structural transformation occurring at the edges of small carbon dots (Fig. 1g and h). The hexagonal carbon ring undergoes a reconstruction, forming a pentagonal ring (Fig. 1i), which is commonly observed in various graphitic materials [17,18], and is recognized as the activity origin for ORR. Moreover, considering the elemental mapping image demonstrating a consistent and uniform distribution of N and S heteroatoms on the carbon substrate, it strongly suggests the possibility of heteroatom doping occurring within the pentagonal ring structure in this study.

To additionally elucidate the specific chemical groups and structures of the catalysts, the raw OPD, CDs-1 and HT-CDs-1 were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (Fig. 2 a). In the FT-IR spectra of OPD, two sharp absorption peaks are clearly observed at 3500∼3300 cm−1, corresponding to the -NH2 functional group attached to its benzene ring. Meanwhile, the sharp peak at this position in both CDs-1 and HT-CDs-1 changes to a broader peak, indicating that a majority of -NH2 groups in OPD were destroyed after hydrothermal treatment, showing a characteristic N-H stretching vibrational peak. The sharp peak at 2830 cm−1 represents the C-H out-of-plane bending vibration. Three distinct spikes with high and uneven intensities at 1600 cm−1, 1500 cm−1 and 1463 cm−1 are evident in both OPD and CDs-1, indicating the presence of the benzene ring skeleton. Despite the emergence of certain defective structures such as pentagons within the benzene ring skeleton after heat treatment, the fundamental C=C structures remain intact. The peak near 750 cm−1 belongs to the C-H out-of-plane bending vibration of the o-disubstituted benzene ring. Based on the above information, it is evident that functional groups such as amino groups and the benzene ring skeleton persist in the catalysts derived from OPD. These functional groups are believed to contribute to favorable hydrophilic properties of CDs. Weak absorption signals in the region of 1050–1166 cm−1 indicate the presence of C=S bonds in all three samples, with a new peak appearing at 607 cm-1 in both CDs-1 and HT-CDs-1, suggesting the formation of C-S bonds [26,31]. Despite the relatively low abundance of both N and S heteroatoms in the material, it is not difficult to find the N-S stretching vibration signal located at 960 cm−1 [32], implying the N-S species probably affect the overall performance of the catalysts [33].

Since the graphitization degree of the catalyst impacts the conductivity and intrinsic activity according to previous studies, a comparative XRD pattern of CDs-1 and HT-CDs-1 is offered in Fig. 2b to estimate their crystallinity [34,35]. Two significant and sharp diffraction peaks appear at the positions around 18° and 26° in the CDs-1, indicating that the CDs are well-oriented and possess relatively good crystallinity. Diffraction peaks of both materials located at around 44° are well-matched with the (101) crystal plane of graphitic carbon. In the XRD patterns of HT-CDs-1, broad peaks are observed around 26° and 44°, corresponding to the (002) and (101) planes of graphitic carbon [36,37]. Compared to the standard PDF card (No. 75–1621) for graphitic carbons, the peak positions are all shifted to the left due to the multi-domain declines π-π interactions, resulting in some sp3 hybridization [38]. The diffraction peak is broad and blunt stemming from the complete destruction of its crystal structure after the high temperature treatment of 900°, generating a large number of intrinsic carbon defects in the process of deformation and reorganization. As a result, the HT-CDs-1 exhibit an amorphous nature and eventually become a typical amorphous carbon structure with only short-range order.

To further quantitatively investigate the degree of defects and graphitization of the catalysts, we characterized the prepared HT-CDs-1, HT-CDs-2 and HT-CDs-3 catalysts by Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 2c). The peaks located around 1350 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 correspond to the characteristic D and G bands, respectively. The D peak is due to the breaking of translational symmetry and the introduction of defects, while the G peak is generated by the first-order scattering of the aromatic structured sp2 lattice [38,39]. The calculated ID/IG results indicate the similar defect density among the three catalysts. This clearly indicates that the catalysts have abundant intrinsic defects and disordered structures consistent with the XRD results. Finally, the N2 adsorption-desorption isothermal curve of HT-CDs-1 was measured in Fig. 2d to characterize its surface area of 1029.47 m2/g and porous structure with the average pore diameter as 3.7 nm, elucidating the water vapor physical activation creates a large number of nano-scaled pores inside the structure of HT-CDs-1.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and linear scanning voltammetry (LSV) tests were performed on various metal-free catalysts using typical three-electrode systems to evaluate their ORR performance and stability in alkaline electrolytes (Fig. 3 ). The oxygen reduction activities of the HT-CDs-1, HT-CDs-2 and HT-CDs-3 catalysts can be visually analyzed from Fig. 3a with half-wave potentials (E
1/2 ) of 0.91 V, 0.83 V and 0.87 V, as well as reaching 5.32 mA cm−2, 5.68 mA cm−2 and 5.15 mA cm−2 for the limiting current density (J L), respectively. The inset shows histograms of the onset potential (E
onset ) at 0.97 V, 0.95 V and 0.92 V. All three catalysts exhibited better ORR performance than commercial Pt/C catalysts (E
onset = 0.93 V, E
1/2 = 0.85 V, J
L = 4.31 mA cm−2). Among them, HT-CDs-1 showed the best ORR activity with a half-wave potential exceeding Pt/C for 60 mV and a Tafel slope (48 mV dec−1) smaller than Pt/C (147 mV dec−1) (Fig. 3b). To further understand the ORR pathway, the RRDE test was continued for HT-CDs-1, HT-CDs-2 and HT-CDs-3. From Fig. 3c and S5 plots, it can be seen that the electron transfer number in the potential range of 0.3 V–0.8 V is close to 4. LSV tests were also performed for each of the three catalysts at different rotation speeds from 400 to 2500 rpm. The corresponding Koutecky-Levich (K-L) curves fitted in the inset show that the three catalysts remain in a four-electron ORR reduction path. These are sufficient to show that the catalysts are efficient in reducing O2 to OH− by four electrons pathway. A series samples of HT-CDs-1 fabricated under different temperatures were also conducted the performance evaluation (Fig. S6). It presents that the sample annealed at 900 °C exhibits the fast kinetics and the largest limiting current density.

To better visualize the ORR activity of the prepared catalysts, a double bar graph was plotted to summarize and compare their kinetic current density (J
k ) and E
1/2 at a potential of 0.85 V (vs. RHE) (Fig. 3d). The J
k of HT-CDs-1 is as high as 19.77 mA cm−2, over four times higher than that of Pt/C (4.32 mA cm−2). The J
k and E
1/2 of some recently reported metal-free ORR catalysts are also compared in Fig. 3e, confirming that HT-CDs-1 is profoundly better than these reported ORR catalysts [14,26,40–43]. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to measure the impedance of this catalyst, and Nyquist plots as in Fig. S7 were drawn to study the interfacial electron transfer dynamics of the catalyst. It can be visualized that the HT-CDs-1 impedance is as large as 218 Ω. In addition, the three HT-CDs were subjected to CV tests at different scan rates (10 mV/s∼100 mV/s) in the non-Faraday interval. The fitted straight line of scan rate versus current density is plotted at a potential of 0.314 V (vs RHE), and HT-CDs-1 achieves the maximum electrochemically active area of 1435 cm2 (Fig. S8). These results indicate that HT-CDs possess low thermodynamics barrier and fast kinetic process. Furthermore, the HT-CDs-1 was tested for a long term i-t curve at a constant voltage (Fig. 3f). The current of the catalyst decayed by just 13 % after more than 5.5 h of cycling, while Pt/C decays by 22 %.

Considering the catalytic properties of metal-free catalysts are dominantly determined by the heteroatoms and structure reconfiguration on the surface, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is inducted (Fig. 4 and S9). As depicted in Fig. S9a, the CDs-1 are mainly composed of five elements (C, N, O, S and Cl) and the atomic content of N (12.82 %) accounts for the highest proportion of all doped elements. Upon high temperature treatment, it can be found that the content of N in the HT-CDs-1 significantly decreases and the content of S and O also decreases to a certain extent. This reduction is attributed to the reaction between water molecules and CDs during the high-temperature process, leading to the release of gases such as CO, CO2, SO2 and other gases [11,18,44]. The C 1s peak of HT-CDs-1 is deconvoluted to four contributions, namely C-C/C=C, C-S, C-O/C-N and O-C=O at the positions of 284.7 eV, 285.2 eV, 286.4 eV and 288.5 eV, respectively (Fig. S9b) [28,45,46]. The results show that the binding energy of C-C/C=C is elevated after high temperature treatment, indicating that the sp2 hybrid graphitic carbon structure is disrupted and some sp3 hybrid carbon defects are produced [31]. In contrast, the decreased binding energies of C-S, C-O/C-N and O-C=O indicate an elevated electron density around C, suggesting not only the presence of electron transfer between C and the hetero-elements (N and S), but also that the edges have been modified by oxygen functional groups. In Fig. S9c and S9d, the XPS high-resolution spectra of CDs before and after the reaction are compared for O and Cl elements are compared. The two fine peaks at 200.37 and 201.98 eV fitted to the XPS spectrum of CDs are the Cl 2p3/2 and Cl 2p1/2 peaks of the C-Cl bond, respectively, indicating covalent doping of Cl atoms into the sp2 hybridized graphitic carbon [47,48]. After high temperature calcination, the percentage of Cl atoms bonded covalently decreased from 0.69 % to 0.09 %, indicating complete removal of Cl atoms.

In order to investigate the role of different hetero-element components in the catalysis, CDs without hetero-element doping, with S-element doping only and with N-element doping only were prepared, respectively. The two single-element doped CDs were characterized by XPS (Fig. S10). It can be visualized from the figure that N-CDs and S-CDs contain only one hetero-element, i.e. N or S, respectively. They were prepared into catalysts using the same reaction conditions and activation methods as those of HT-CDs, and their ORR performance and Tafel slopes were tested, respectively (Fig. 4a and b). The results show that CDs doped with one hetero-element can only increase their potentials by the same activation and heat treatment, but there is still a great deal of difficulty in boosting the currents. Inversely, HT-CDs-1 with N, S elements exhibited superior ORR activity and the lowest Tafel slope (48 mV dec−1). The XPS data further demonstrates that the chemical environment and structure around N and S, as shown in Fig. 4c and d, S11 and S12. Three characterized peaks are fitted to the N 1s survey spectrum of CDs-1, namely pyridinic N, -NH2 and graphitic N at positions 398.1 eV, 399.4 eV and 401.3 eV, respectively. The N 1s deconvolution of HT-CDs-1 is divided into pyridinic N (398.7 eV), pyrrolic N (399.9 eV), graphitic-centered N (401.0 eV), graphitic-valleyed N (401.9 eV), and oxidized N (403.5 eV) [49,50]. Due to the spin coupling of the electronic orbitals of S 2p, the binding energy of CDs-1 is fitted to five peaks as thiophene S at 163.5 eV and 164.74 eV, while oxidized S at 166.01 eV, 168.06 eV, and 169.28 eV, respectively [7]. The positions of S 2p peaks, especially of the thiophene S in HT-CDs-1, are shifted apparently toward the higher binding energy, indicating a significant electron loss. Since N atom displays a higher electronegativity than C atom, implying that the atom structure transformation from S-C to S-N during the annealing contributes to the binding energy increase of S in HT-CDs, corresponded well to the FT-IR results.

The relative contents of the different characterized peaks of N 1s and S 2p in the three samples (HT-CDs-X) were depicted in comparative histograms to elucidate the ORR catalytic mechanism (Fig. 4e and f). The N histogram illustrates predominant conversion to graphitic N following high-temperature pyrolysis, indicating enhanced overall graphitization. At the same time, a portion of oxidized N is produced in the catalyst, which is due to the reaction of oxygen molecules with CDs in water vapor. Regarding S contents, a significant reduction in oxidized S occurs after high temperature annealing, while the thiophene S content increases. Analysis of chemical bonds using FT-IR and XPS confirms that the presence of thiophene S and graphitic N primarily influences ORR activity [7].

Considering the connection between graphitic N and thiophene S through N-S bonds, we have established two probable active site models (C-S-N and N-S-N) for HT-CDs and an individual S dopant model (C-S-C) as shown in Fig. 5 a. The charge distribution simulation clearly displayed the heterogeneous distribution of the positive and negative charges. Specifically, in the C-S-C model, the electrons are predominantly located around the S atom, while in the other models the electrons prefer to accumulate on the aromatic nucleus upon N dopant (Fig. 5b). Due to the reduction reaction activity is to a large extent determined by the active valence electrons on the reaction sites, rather than the overall electrons, it is essential to figure out the charge filling states on the HOMO and LUMO orbitals (Fig. 5c and d). Interestingly, in the two models with N doping, the valence electrons accumulate on the S site. In the C-S-C model holes tend to aggregate on the S atom, contrary to the situation of the overall charge distributions. The DFT calculations further indicate the electron fillings in the valence electron orbitals (MO) [51,52].The electron configuration in the valence MO of the above three models are illustrated in Fig. 5e. In the C-S-C model, electrons fill up the bonding orbital while leaving the anti-bonding orbital empty, leading to the strong binding between the C and S. On the contrary, when C is substituted by graphitic N, the πg anti-bonding orbital is partially occupied by one electron for C-S-N. On the N-S-N model, the πg anti-bonding orbital is filled by the paired electrons. Since the electrons in the πg anti-bonding orbital with higher energy level enable the efficient heterogeneous charge transfer from the catalyst to the reactant O2 and the unpaired electrons are more active than the paired electrons during the catalysis, the activity of the three models can be deduced as C-S-N > N-S-N > C-S-C. The calculated free energy profiles also verify this deduction that the ΔG of C-S-N model is 0.6 eV, smaller than the N-S-N model of 0.74 eV and C-S-C model of 1.93 eV (Fig. S13). This underscores the significant role of the synergistic effect between nitrogen and sulfur, amplifying the improvement in ORR performance far beyond those achievable with individual heteroatom doping.

In view of the excellent ORR activity of HT-CDs-1, we assembled a rechargeable zinc-air cell using a carbon cloth coated with this catalyst as the air cathode (Fig. 6 ). Fig. 6a shows the open-circuit voltage (OCPT) of the assembled zinc-air cell, which is stable at around 1.42 V for 400 s, demonstrating its capability for high voltage output. The step discharge curve in Fig. 6b reveals that the voltage at 1 mA cm−2 is approximately 1.28 V, and the voltage retains at about 1.25 V after waiting for stabilization. Fig. 6c presents the power density and current density of the HT-CDs-1 based cell achieving a maximum power density of up to 63 mW cm−2. Fig. 6d illustrates the constant current discharge curves at different current densities, indicating initial discharge potentials of 1.29 V at 2 mA cm−2, 1.27 V at 5 mA cm−2 and 1.24 V at 10 mA cm−2. Specific energies calculated from the zinc consumption were 551.3 Wh kg−1, 635.6 Wh kg−1, and 813.6 Wh kg−1, respectively. Finally, for practical applications, the charge/discharge cycle performance of the zinc-air battery assembled with HT-CDs-1 catalyst was evaluated, as shown in Fig. 6e. The charge/discharge cycle time of this battery was 108 h, with a round-trip cycle efficiency of 63.0 % after 10 h. As the charge/discharge cycle time increases, the polarization of the cell becomes more pronounced, exhibiting a slight decrease in round-trip cycle efficiency to 58.4 % after 100 h at 1.998 V. Comparatively, the zinc-air battery assembled by Pt/C catalyst only sustains the activity by 46 h (Fig. S14). Furthermore, the lifetime of battery at large current density of 10 mA cm−2 was also conducted and displayed 23 h inferior to the lifetime under low current density (Fig. S15). These results indicate the assembled zinc-air batteries possess good long-term durability and potential applications.

Lin Wu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Data curation. Chunyu Ji: Data curation. Xueyan Li: Data curation. Zirui Liu: Data curation. Hao Yang: Data curation. Feng Qiu: Supervision. Zhili Peng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Qingju Liu: Supervision. Longzhou Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52362043; 22001228), the “Double-First Class” University Construction Project (No. C176220100022) and the Yunnan Fundamental Research Project (No. 202201AT070227; 202301AT070147; 202301BF070001-003). Longzhou Zhang also thanks Xingdian Youth Talent Support Program of Yunnan Province (YNQR-QNRC-2018-056). The authors would like to thank the Advance Analysis and Measurement Center of Yunnan University and the Advanced Computing Center of Yunnan University.
